def: The set of beliefs you bring to research about what is real and how we come to know things. It shapes the whole way you design your study.
Your beliefs about what reality is. For example, whether there is one objective reality or many realities shaped by people's experiences.
Your beliefs about how we know what we know — whether knowledge is discovered by measuring the world, or built through people's interpretations.
The role your values and ethics play in the research — how much your beliefs influence what you study and how.
A worldview that says there is one objective, measurable reality. Positivists use numbers and experiments to test ideas.
Like positivism, but accepts that we can never measure reality perfectly — only get close through careful, repeated research.
A worldview that says reality is built through people's experiences and social contexts. Constructivists explore meanings rather than measure them.
A worldview focused on social justice — research aimed at understanding and improving the lives of marginalized or under-served groups.
A flexible worldview that chooses whatever methods (numbers, words, or both) best answer the research question.
The general subject or issue you want to study, narrowed enough to be manageable.
The specific issue, gap, or difficulty your study will address — the 'why this matters'.
Your own reasons for doing the study — curiosity, career, or personal experiences that motivate you.
Real-world outcomes you hope the research will achieve, such as improving a practice or informing a decision.
What you want to understand or learn — the knowledge or insight the study should produce.
A summary and analysis of what other researchers have already found about your topic. It shows what's known and where the gaps are.
Existing studies directly about your subject that help justify why your topic is worth researching.
An established theory you use as a lens to guide and interpret your study.
A map of the key concepts in your study and how you think they connect. It ties together your worldview, topic, literature, and questions.
A question that existing research hasn't answered yet — the space your study will help fill.
Based on observed or measured evidence (data) rather than only on opinion or theory.
Your overall plan and reasoning for how you will study your question — quantitative, qualitative, or mixed.
Research that collects and analyzes numerical data to measure, compare, or test relationships.
Research that explores experiences, meanings, and ideas using words — from interviews, observations, or texts.
Combining both quantitative (numbers) and qualitative (words) approaches in one study.
The specific structure of your study — for example experimental, correlational, case study, or ethnography.
A study where you change one thing (a variable) and measure the effect, often using control and comparison groups.
A study that measures whether two or more things are related, without changing anything.
A study that describes the characteristics of a group or situation as it is.
An in-depth study of a single person, group, or situation to understand it deeply.
A qualitative design where the researcher immerses in a culture or group to understand it from the inside.
A qualitative design that studies the lived experience of people around a particular event or idea.
A qualitative design that builds a new theory directly from the patterns found in the data.
Research done to solve a real, practical problem through cycles of acting and reflecting, often by practitioners themselves.
The clear, focused question your study is designed to answer.
A testable, specific prediction about what you expect to find, usually in quantitative studies.
The prediction that there is NO relationship or difference — what you test against.
Anything that can change or take different values in a study, such as age, score, or method.
The factor you change or compare — the presumed cause.
The outcome you measure — what may be affected by the independent variable.
Defining exactly how you will measure a concept (e.g., measuring 'stress' with a specific questionnaire).
The process of gathering the information you'll analyze — through surveys, interviews, observations, or records.
The entire group you want your findings to apply to.
The smaller group you actually collect data from, chosen to represent the population.
The method you use to select your sample from the population.
Choosing participants by chance so everyone has an equal shot — helps make the sample representative.
Deliberately choosing participants who have the specific knowledge or experience you need.
Selecting participants who are easiest to reach. Quick, but may be less representative.
A set of questions given to many people to gather data, often producing numbers.
A conversation with participants used to gather detailed, in-their-own-words data.
Making sense of your collected data to answer your research question.
Numbers that summarize data, such as averages, percentages, and ranges.
Statistics that let you draw conclusions about a whole population from your sample.
Three kinds of 'average': the mean is the total divided by the count, the median is the middle value, the mode is the most common value.
A measure of how strongly two things move together. Note: correlation does not prove one causes the other.
A statistical method for predicting one variable from one or more others.
A qualitative method that finds and organizes recurring themes across your data.
Labeling pieces of qualitative data (like interview quotes) so you can group and analyze them.
Using multiple methods or sources to cross-check findings and strengthen trust in them.
Whether your study actually measures what it claims to measure.
Whether your measurements are consistent and would give similar results if repeated.
How confident you can be that the cause you claim really produced the effect, not something else.
How well your findings can be generalized to other people, places, or times.
Whether your measurement truly captures the abstract idea (construct) you intend, like 'motivation'.
The qualitative version of validity — how believable and well-supported your findings are.
How well qualitative findings might apply to other settings, given enough detail for readers to judge.
Whether the qualitative research process is consistent and could be followed by others.
The degree to which qualitative findings come from the data, not the researcher's bias.
Anything that unfairly tilts your results away from the truth.
The principles that keep research honest, fair, and safe for everyone involved.
Getting participants' clear agreement to take part after explaining what the study involves and any risks.
Keeping participants' information private and protected.
Collecting data so that no one — not even the researcher — can link responses to a specific person.
An Institutional Review Board that reviews studies to make sure they protect participants.
The ethical duty to maximize benefits and minimize harm to participants.
